items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).
Students use magnetism as the test, but stainless steel (ferrous) can be non-magnetic. The defining property is iron content - 'ferrous' comes from the Latin for iron.

items.q: What is the difference between a wood stain and a varnish?
items.correct: Stain colours the wood; varnish provides a protective surface coat.
items.random_wrong: Stain is permanent, varnish always peels off.
items.why: Students confuse colouring (stain penetrates) with protecting (varnish sits on top). Many products combine both, but their primary functions differ. Often stain is applied first, then varnish.

items.q: Plywood is strong in all directions because veneers are glued:
items.why: Correct answer: At 90°. “Parallel” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which board has a smooth, grain-free face ideal for painting?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: MDF. “OSB” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An MDF sheet is 2440 × 1220 mm. Approximate area in m² (to 1 dp)?
items.why: The real figure is 3.0. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
